headline: Permissionless on-chain deposits/withdrawals via public submit() and requestWithdrawals(); passive ToS eligibility only; direct contract access documented

findings.text: No whitelist/allowlist modifiers (e.g. onlyWhitelisted, onlyRole, isAccredited) or KYC checks in user-facing entry points. Lido contract submit() and Withdrawal Queue requestWithdrawals/claimWithdrawals are public with no gating (Etherscan verified source/ABI at 0xae7ab96520DE3A18E5e111B5EaAb095312D7fE84 and 0x889edC2eDab5f40e902b864aD4d7AdE8E412F9B1).

No off-chain operators (keepers, privileged relayers, oracles) required to admit user actions. Deposits via submit() and withdrawal requests are unconditional on-chain; any oracle dependency for finalization is liveness-only and deferred to dependencies slice (confirmed via contract code inspection and transaction history on Etherscan).

Official frontend (lido.fi / stake.lido.fi) shows only A3-passive boilerplate ToS clauses (eligibility, sanctions attestation, restricted-territory self-certification, comply-with-law); no A3-active enforcement (no IP geo-blocking, wallet screening via Chainalysis/TRM, KYC wall, or rendering-blocking banner) observed or reported in public sources. No evidence of type (a)-(d) for active enforcement.

A3b-ii independent paths exist and are documented: direct contract interaction via Etherscan/CLI/SDK/wallets (explicitly guided in docs.lido.fi/integrations/wallets and deployed-contracts); third-party frontends and wallet integrations available. A3b-i redistributions (if any) remain bound by same ToS; only A3b-ii count as mitigation.
No on-chain sanctions/compliance tooling or updatable blocklist in core contracts (Lido or Withdrawal Queue source inspection shows zero OFAC-style checks or privileged blocklist functions).

Read access fully permissionless (anyone can query state on Etherscan or via RPC). Write access (deposit via submit(), withdrawal request/claim) also permissionless for all users; no distinction gating write to privileged addresses.

ToS located at https://lido.fi/terms-of-use; references Section 7 eligibility requirements and sanctions/compliance obligations (standard DeFi passive clauses). Verbatim full Section 7 text not retrieved due to fetch limitations.

steelman.red: Protocol deserves red because its ToS explicitly references eligibility requirements in Section 7 and sanctions compliance that could gate users from restricted jurisdictions, combined with upgradeable contracts that could theoretically introduce future whitelists via DAO vote.
steelman.orange: Protocol deserves orange because while contracts admit users unconditionally, the official frontend ToS imposes jurisdictional and sanctions self-certification that may deter or legally restrict users in certain territories, and independent A3b-ii paths, though documented, remain primarily technical and not equally accessible to all users.
steelman.green: Protocol deserves green because core user entry/exit functions (submit() on Lido contract 0xae7ab96520DE3A18E5e111B5EaAb095312D7fE84 and requestWithdrawals/claimWithdrawals on Withdrawal Queue 0x889edC2eDab5f40e902b864aD4d7AdE8E412F9B1) are fully permissionless with zero contract-level whitelist/KYC/operator approval, frontend restrictions are only passive boilerplate ToS, and direct-contract plus third-party access paths are explicitly documented and functional.

rationale.verdict: Choosing green because the on-chain evidence from block explorers (Etherscan code/ABI/transaction history for both staking and withdrawal contracts) and documentation overwhelmingly demonstrates unconditional admission for deposits and withdrawals with no privileged actor or on-chain gate required; passive ToS clauses are standard across DeFi and explicitly compatible with green per the rubric, while A3-active enforcement is absent and alternatives reinforce accessibility. The red and orange steel-mans rely on hypothetical future changes or policy interpretations that lack current supporting evidence in deployed contracts or observed frontend behavior.

evidence.shows: Lido staking contract (stETH proxy) source/ABI: submit() is public payable with no access modifiers, whitelist, KYC, or role checks; fully permissionless deposit path; upgradeable proxy but no pausable/user-gating logic visible.

Withdrawal Queue contract: requestWithdrawals() and claimWithdrawals() are public with no access control modifiers, roles, or gating; permissionless user exit path confirmed via ABI and recent transaction examples from arbitrary addresses.

Official documentation explicitly guides direct contract interaction: call submit(_referral) on Lido contract with ETH value; provides ABI snippet and code examples for permissionless staking without frontend.
Official ToS page; references Section 7 eligibility requirements and sanctions/compliance obligations (passive clauses only; no active enforcement mechanism described).
Canonical index of deployed contract addresses per chain, confirming Ethereum mainnet addresses used in assessment.
Primary source repository for Lido on Ethereum smart contracts (core staking logic); confirms open-source nature and absence of user-facing whitelist logic in main contracts.
Public bug bounty program covering smart contracts and applications (max $2M), confirming security disclosure channel.
Primary governance discussion forum for Lido DAO proposals and community input.

unknowns: A6: exact verbatim text of Section 7 eligibility/sanctions clauses from https://lido.fi/terms-of-use could not be extracted (page fetch returned insufficient content); only high-level references and standard DeFi phrasing confirmed via search snippets.
